title: L1C01 Air Law
notes:
Instructor Notes
Studying regulations may be considered the ‘driest’ and is the longest section of this course. This is why it is being covered first while the students are fresh.

Key Points
Operators and pilots are responsible for being aware of and following current regulations. 
E.g., What regulations are we operating under? What are the rules?  What are the limits?
New regulations have been implemented and regulations change over time. 

Activities
N/A

title: Objectives

content.text:
After this section, you will be able to...
Identify which parts of the Canadian Aviation Regulations apply to RPAS operations
Differentiate between Advanced RPAS operations and Level 1 Complex (L1C) RPAS operations
Explain the difference between a regulation and a standard

notes:
Instructor Notes
These key learning objectives will be covered during this section. 

Key Points
N/A

Activities
N/A

title: Advanced vs. L1C vs. SFOC
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Canada has three operational environments above the 'basic' level: Advanced, Level 1 Complex, and the SFOC environment.
In this course, we will be covering the information required for the L1C certification.

Activities
Discussion: What types of drone operations do each of the participants engage in? Which of three operations environments would apply?

title: L1C Requirements

content.text: Candidates must...

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Participants must meet these requirements for the L1C certificate:
Be minimum of 18 years of age.
Complete the advanced exam, L1C exam, and application online through Transport Canada's Drone Management Portal (DMP).
Complete a 20-hr ground school and in-person flight review (Note: This course meets this requirement).
Ground school instructors must be affiliated with an organization that has declared to Transport Canada that they have a L1C training program that meets the TP 15530 standard.
Flight reviewers must hold a L1C RPAS certificate with flight reviewer privileges and be associated with a TP 15530 declared training organization in the DMP.
The completion order of these requirements is fixed, because Transport Canada wants applicants to build a foundation of knowledge.

Activities
N/A

title: Aviation Regulations

content.text:
The Aeronautics Act:
Is the legislation that governs aviation in Canada
Enables the Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs)

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
The Aeronautics Act from 1985 is what enables the Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs).
The Aeronautics Act still exists, but CARs started in the mid 1990s. CARs was a big change to the existing regulations.

Activities
N/A

title: Main Three Agencies
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Transport Canada:
Builds and enforces regulations
Before the 1990s, provided air traffic control and airport management, but have since privatized that and now oversee public safety in a regulatory capacity
NAV CANADA:
Air traffic control
Does not build regulations
Relevant when flying in the airspace near airports
Transportation Safety Board of Canada:
Independent agency with mandate to review incidents and accidents involving aviation, rail, motorways, and maritime
Make recommendations to Transport Canada and federal government on how to improve regulations for aviation safety
Only investigate incidents with drones over 25kg

Activities
N/A

title: Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs)

content.text:
Part I General Provisions
Part II Aircraft Registration
Part III Airports
Part IV Licensing and Training
Part V Airworthiness
Part VI Flight Rules
Part VII Commercial Air Services
Part VIII Air Navigation Services
Part IX RPAS
Part X Greenhouse Gas Emissions

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
CARs are not only for drones.
Drone operators mostly work with CARs Part 9 but should be familiar with other parts, especially 1 and 6.
Part 1: Introductions of the law
Part 2: Aircraft registration, excluding RPAS registration (which is covered in Part 9)
Part 3: Airport operators must be familiar with these regulatory targets; registered vs. certified (under Part 3 of CARs) air drone
Part 4: Licensing and training for traditional aviation (RPAS training and certification is under PARX IX
Part 5: Type certification of traditional aircraft
Part 6: relevant, because we share space with traditional pilots and want to understand what larger aircraft are going to be doing.
Part 7: Commercial air services (e.g., Air Canada, West Jet, etc.) regulations
Part 8: Air traffic control
Part 9: Most important for drones. Will be discussed in other slides.
Part 10: Greenhouse Gas Emissions (not relevant to RPAS operations)

Activities
N/A

title: Regulation vs. Standard
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Regulation: Can be enforced
Standard: Method to enable compliance with regulation
The regulation states the rule, and the standard explains how to meet that rule.

Activities
N/A

title: Numbering Structure

content.text:
First digit identifies the Part.
For example:
901.56: Part IX

Second digit identifies a regulation versus a standard.
For example:
901.56  ‘0’ = regulation
921.04  ‘2’ = standard

Third digit identifies the Subpart.
For example:
901.56: Subpart 1 of Part IX of the CARs

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
First digit: Part
Second digit: Regulation or standard; a 0 signifies a regulation and a 2 signifies a standard.
Third digit: Subpart; there are two subparts in Part 9.
SFOCs are managed under Subpart 3 of CARs. 

Activities
N/A

title: CARs Part I – General Provisions

content.text: Provides a comprehensive list of definitions used in the Canadian Aviation Regulations (CAR 101.01)

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
It is important to be familiar with the definitions to ensure everyone is speaking the same language.
For example, in CARs Part 9, an RPAS operator refers to the individual or organization holding the RPAS Operator Certificate, not the pilot.

Activities
N/A

title: Part I – General Provisions

content.text:
Remotely Piloted Aircraft (RPA)A navigable aircraft, other than a balloon, rocket, or kite, that is operated by a pilot who is not on board
Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS)A set of configurable elements consisting of an RPA, its control station, the command and control links, and any other system elements required during flight operation

Command and Control LinkThe data link between an RPA and a control station that is used in the management of a flight
Control StationThe collection of facilities and equipment from which an RPA is controlled and monitored

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
RPAS vs RPA
RPAS: Refers to the whole system, including the command station, command and control link, and the aircraft
RPA: The aircraft itself
Control Station: Collection of facilities and equipment. It is up to the manufacturer to decide the level of the control station (e.g., RC controller, laptop, big moving van, etc.).
CARs Part 9 does not differentiate model aircraft and drones. They are all covered.

Activities
N/A

title: Part I – General Provisions

content.text: CAR 103.01 Document Inspection

…shall produce the Canadian aviation document, technical record, or other document for inspection in accordance with the terms of a demand made by a peace officer, an immigration officer, or the Minister
CAR 103.04 Record Keeping

Digital: Protected against loss/destruction/tampering
May be printed on paper and provided to the Minister with reasonable notice

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Transport Canada has been asking and training law enforcement agencies to assist in having a basic understanding of drone regulations, so they can intervene with nefarious drone operations and understand what is legitimate and what is noncompliant.
Drone operators must provide records to law enforcement, immigration, or the Minister upon request. 
Records must be kept and stored digitally or physically. If storing digitally, you must have a backup. 

Activities
N/A

title: 106.01 Part I – Accountable Executive

content.text: Appointment and Acceptance

…to be responsible for operations or activities authorized under the certificate and accountable on their behalf for meeting the requirements of these Regulations…
…no person shall be appointed… unless they have control of the financial and human resources…

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
According to Transport Canada, an accountable executive is someone who has control of the financial and human resources in the company. 
This role is typically occupied by the president but does not have to be.
If a single person operates with an RPAS Operator Certificate, they occupy all roles (person responsible for maintenance, accountable executive, pilot, etc.)
A requirement of an RPAS Operator Certificate is to define an organizational structure, including who the accountable executive is.

Activities
Discussion: What is the participant’s organization structure? Who is the accountable executive? 	 OR What would be the structure if the organization had an operating certificate? Who would be the accountable executive?

title: Part VI – General Operating & Flight Rules
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
N/A

Activities
N/A

title: CARs Part VI – Flight Rules – Traditional Aircraft

content.text: Visual Flight Rules (VFR)

Visual reference to the surface of the earth
Visual Meteorological Conditions (VMC)
‘See and be seen’ 
Typical for small aircraft sharing the same airspace as RPAS
Instrument Flight Rules (IFR)

No longer require reference to the surface on the Earth
Airlines and aircraft that fly at high altitudes
Near airports/aerodromes

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Since airspace is shared, drone operators and pilots must have a basic understanding of what traditional aircraft are doing in the airspace.
Part VI of the CARS has two groups of rules: VFR and IFR for flying an aircraft.
VFR: Flying in visual reference to the ground (out of cloud, can see the ground, heads outside of cockpit); NAV CANADA is not looking out for drones, so pilots must look out and be reactive (see and be seen). ‘See and be seen’ is old technology that the industry should try to move away from, because a pilot’s vision can fail.
IFR: No longer flying in visual reference to the ground; set of rules needed when flying in and above cloud (flying on instruments); NAV CANADA gets everyone out of the drone’s way.

Activities
N/A

title: Canadian Domestic Airspace (CDA)

content.text:
Canadian Domestic Airspace:
Southern Domestic Airspace (SDA)
Northern Domestic Airspace (NDA)
Canadian Regulations (CARs) apply to this airspace
Different rules between SDA and NDA
NDA: ARC-A (atypical) airspace for RPAS operators

notes:
Instructor Notes
NAV CANADA’s Designated Airspace Handbook shows the SDA and NDA on a map.
Highlight how north the NDA is.

Key Points
Most Canadian operations will be in the SDA.
There is a different set of rules for the NDA, because it is very low risk and low traffic.
In NDA, everything below 400 ft AGL is atypical airspace (ARC A), which means the DAA performance criteria has significantly reduced.
For the rest of this course, “airspace” is going to refer to SDA.

Activities
N/A

title: Altimeter Setting vs. Standard Pressure Regions
notes:
Instructor Notes
This will be discussed more in the Systems slide deck.

Key Points
Pilots have two general regions: Altimeter Setting Region and Standard Pressure Region.
When operating in SDA, drones are in an Altimeter Setting Region. 
When operating near an airport, there is an altimeter setting that you must put into the altimeter to make your altitude real (or to make it as close to a true altitude as possible). Those altitudes are corrected to an altitude above sea level.
When operating in NDA, drones are in a Standard Pressure Region.
The weather stations are so far apart, so pilots can’t get all the corrections needed to have a valid altimeter reading.
When outside of takeoff and landing, set a standard pressure of 2992.
The altitudes are part of traffic management in traditional aviation and work towards traffic separation.
It’s important to work on common altitudes for separation.

Activities
Practice Question: What are RPAS altitudes derived from?
Answer: Your drone is giving you true altitude. Your aircraft altitude is not a true altitude unless at are at a standard ISA temperature. Anything that deviates outside of ISA and traditional aircraft are no longer at a true altitude. 
Discussion: What are the challenges combining RPAS (GPS) altitudes with traditional aircraft that use altimeters?

Instructor Notes
The importance of this slide is to discuss the many different altitude references that exist in RPAS.

Key Points
Drones typically refer to home altitude. Pilots need to understand that altitude and the unit it is working in.
Eventually, drones will need to ‘speak the same language’ as traditional aircraft, so drone operators should develop procedures that work in the same ‘language’ as traditional aircraft.
Understanding how altitude works in the ground control station is step one in being able to develop procedures.

Activities
N/A

title: Requirements by Airspace Class IFR
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
For the RPAS Operator Certificate/L1C operations, drones will be operating in the Class F and Class G airspace for the most part.
Drone operators do not need to understand the nuances of Class A, B, C, D, and E.
It is more important to understand if the airspace is controlled or uncontrolled.

Activities
N/A

title: Requirements by Airspace Class VFR
notes:
Instructor Notes
Speak about weather limitations.

Key Points
It is important to understand the ceiling and visibility requirements for helicopters when flying in Class G uncontrolled airspace.
In Class G airspace, an aircraft must have either 
2 miles of visibility and clear cloud or 
1 mile visibility, can’t be more than 500 ft vertical separation to the cloud and 2000 ft horizontal separation to the cloud
These requirements have a lot to do with reaction time (see and be seen).

Activities
N/A

title: Airspace Structure

content.text: Vertical Profile

notes:
Instructor Notes
The airspace is sometimes referred to as an upside-down wedding cake.

Key Points
This is what the vertical profile of airspace looks like.
Around airports, there are transition errors (that usually start around 700 ft AGL), Class C control zones, and controlled areas for IFR traffic.
There are some areas of Class F (special use) airspace that may occasionally become Class G airspace.
In the L1C world, drones will be operating in the green area (Class G airspace).

Activities
N/A

title: AC 903-001 7.0 Air Risk Determination
notes:
Instructor Notes
This was not reviewed in Advanced RPAS training.

Key Points
ARC stands for Air Risk Class.
In BVLOS under the L1C, you will be operating under ARC-A, -B, and occasionally -C.
ARC-D airspace is going to be handled under the SFOC process. NAV CANADA will get involved, because almost all ARC-D airspace is regulated by NAV CANADA.

Activities
N/A

title: AC 903-001 7.0 Air Risk Determination
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
This flowchart helps calculate the ARC.
The ARC determines the performance of the technology-based DAA system: Higher ARCs require more robust performance.
ARC-A is performance requirements for DAA are almost nonexistent. There is almost no requirement to have an onboard DAA to meet the requirements for ARC-A.

Activities
N/A

title: Controlled vs. Uncontrolled Airspace
notes:
Instructor Notes
Risk ratios and criteria are discussed later.

Key Points
For the most part, operators only need to understand if the airspace is controlled or uncontrolled.
Class F is mostly uncontrolled but occasionally could be controlled.
Class A, B, C, D, E are controlled.

Activities
N/A

title: Designation of Class F Airspace

content.text:
Example CYA113(A):
CYA113(A)
CY = Canada
CYA113(A)
A = Alert
D = Danger
R = Restricted
CYA113(A)
Area identification

CYA114(A)
A = Acrobatic
F = Aircraft Test Area
H = Hang Gliding
M = Military
P = Parachuting
S = Soaring
T = Training

notes:
Instructor Notes
This may not have been covered in the Advanced RPAS exam, but some of these questions may be on the L1C exam.
Remind the students that questions related to airspace designation may in the L1C online exam.

Key Points
All Class F airspace is going to have a designation, using an alphanumeric designator.
The first two letters  (CY) indicate Canada.
The third letter indicates the type of Class F airspace.
The three numbers indicate the designation (area identification) of the Class F airspace.
The modifier after the designator indicates the type or use of the special use airspace.

Activities
N/A

title: Where to Find Airspace Dimensions?

content.text:
Designated Airspace Handbook
Includes user/controlling agency for Class F airspace (CYA, CYR)
Effective dates
‘Ultimate authority’

notes:
Instructor Notes
Participants should have a laptop and internet access during this course to access the Designated Airspace Handbook.

Key Points
Find information on this in the Designated Airspace Handbook, which is free to download.
This handbook is the ultimate authority and law for the regulations when building programs and looking at a new site assessment, because it is regularly updated.

Activities
Practice Question: Can you find the Designated Airspace Handbook? 
Answer: All participants can locate the handbook online.

title: Where to Find Airspace Dimensions?

content.text:
NAV Drone Map Viewer
NRCAN Drone Site Selection Tool - 2
VFR Navigation Charts (VNC)
Effective dates

Freemium providers
SkyVector
FltPlan Go
Commercial providers
Foreflight
FlySafe

notes:
Instructor Notes
Show participants how to find and use NRCAN DSST-2 later.
Participants will likely need to look at a VNC map on their L1C exam.

Key Points
NAV Drone:
Has a lot of airspace information and has a geographic representation of NOTAM closed areas, which not a lot of providers do
Alberta has a lot of closed airspace due to wildfires. NAV Drone is helpful for verifying these areas when operating
NRCAN Drone Site Selection Tool (DSST-2):
Can be used for BVLOS operations
Has already accounted for population density
If the area is shaded, don’t fly. If the area is not shaded, fly (L1C operations).
Freemium Providers:
FltPlan Go is a good provider for finding the Canada Flight Supplement (CFS) airport information and VNC maps.
Commercial Providers:
Foreflight is the standard in traditional aviation and is user friendly.

Activities
N/A

title: Where to Find Airport Information?

content.text:
Canada Flight Supplement
Canada Water Aerodrome Supplement
NRCAN Drone Site Selection Tool – 2
VFR Navigation Charts (VNC)
Effective dates

Commercial providers
Foreflight'
Flysafe
Freemium providers
FltPlan Go (free)
Sky Vector (free)

notes:
Instructor Notes
Participants will refer to Canada Flight Supplement for their advanced online ground school.

Key Points
Canada Flight Supplement:
Regularly updated. Do not buy paper copies, because they will become outdated quickly.
Most of the data contained in the CFS is also included with apps such as ForeFlight and FltPlanGo.
L1C operations cannot occur within 5 nautical miles of an aerodrome listed in in this document.
DSST-2 tool has built the 5 nautical mile buffers around these aerodromes.
NAV Drone also includes a layer to visualize the 5 nautical mile buffers around the aerodromes listed in the CFS.
Aerodromes in this document are considered 'registered'.  However, commercial airports may also be considered certified (cert) as meeting CARs Part III requirements for an airport.
There is also a 'Canada Water Aerodrome Supplement.’

Activities
N/A

title: Part VI – Division V – 601.96 Operations at or in the Vicinity of an Aerodrome

content.text:
Left-hand pattern 1,000 ft AGL, except:
Published in the CFS, or
Authorized by ATC
Maintain listening watch on appropriate frequency

notes:
Instructor Notes
This was covered in more depth in the Advanced RPAS course.

Key Points
You must fly a left-hand traffic pattern of 1,000 ft AGL, unless published otherwise in the Canada Flight Supplement or authorized by air traffic control.
Airports in the traffic pattern must maintain a listening watch on the appropriate frequency.

Activities
N/A

title: Part VI – Division V – 601.96 Operations at or in the Vicinity of an Aerodrome

content.text:
Transient aircraft will remain 2,000 ft AGL above unless:
Public safety
Aerial inspection
Full list at 602.96 (5)

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Transient aircraft over air drones must fly 2,000 ft AGL, so they don’t conflict with traffic patterns.
Altitudes are one criteria used in traffic separation, so it is important to know at what altitudes aircrafts are operating.

Activities
N/A

title: Part VI – Division V 601.97 Uncontrolled Aerodromes within an MF Area

content.text:
Reporting on departure (602.100):
Before: Broadcast intentions and look and listen
After: Broadcast leaving
Reporting on arrival (602.101):
Broadcast 5 minutes prior to arrival
Broadcast traffic pattern location
Broadcast clear on the ground

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
A lot of the same criteria that applies to uncontrolled aerodromes applies here too.
Broadcast intentions 5 minutes prior to arrival.
Air operators for public safety, life rescue, or aerial inspection will have exceptions that allow them to fly below these regulations.

Activities
Practice Question: How low can a traditional aircraft fly legally?
Answer: As low as needed if there is a requirement to be that low.  Slide 34 will explain CAR 602.15 low altitude flight.

title: Part VI – Division I – 601.14 Minimum Altitudes & Distances
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Drones must fly higher when above a built-up area/open-air assembly.

Activities
Practice Question: What is the definition of a built-up area?
Answer: There is no official definition of what a built-up area is. This is a gap in the regulations.

title: Part VI – Division I – 602.15 Permissible Low Altitude Flight

content.text:
Altitudes can be flown lower than those listed in 602.14 for:
Police
Saving human life
Firefighting/ambulance
Fisheries Act
Administration of national or provincial parks

Flight inspection
Aerial application
Aerial photography
Helicopter external loads
Flight training

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There are exceptions to the altitudes that can be flown.

Activities
N/A

title: 601.15 CARs Forest Fire Aircraft Operating Restrictions

content.text:
No person shall operate an aircraft:
(a) over a forest fire area, or over any area that is located within 5 nautical miles of a forest fire area, at an altitude of less than 3,000 ft AGL; or
(b) in any airspace that is described in a NOTAM issued pursuant to section 601.16.

notes:
Instructor Notes
Example incidents when a drone has caused a suspicion to flight operations:
www.ctvnews.ca/edmonton/article/man-fined-10k-for-flying-drone-during-wildfire-fighting-efforts-in-jasper-national-park 
www.ctvnews.ca/world/article/canadian-super-scooper-plane-grounded-after-hitting-civilian-drone-over-los-angeles-wildfires 

Key Points
Wildfire operations are frequently suspended, because a drone was flying too close. 
Even a traditional aircraft can’t fly near a wildfire (within 5 nautical miles and at an altitude of less than 3,000 ft AGL).
Drone operators are required to avoid wildfire areas even if there is no closed NOTAM space (designated and undesignated).
Traditional air pilots are required to report wildfires.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – RPAS
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
CARs Part IX includes the most important regulations for drone operators.
There have been a lot of changes in this new body of rules.

Activities
N/A

title: Timeline of Part IX to CARs

content.text: Prior to 2019, Transport Canada used the SFOC process for RPAS operations.
CARs Part IX
New part of the Canadian Aviation Regulations to address small remotely piloted aircraft systems. SFOC process manages operations not addressed by Part IX.
NPA Lower-Risk BVLOS
Notice of proposed amendments to CARs Part IX to address low-risk BVLOS.
Canada Gazette, Part I Lower-Risk BVLOS
Proposed changes to CARs Part IX to consolidate low-risk BVLOS operations.
Canada Gazette, Part II Lower-Risk BVLOS
Enactment of changes to CARs Part IX, fully coming into effect November 4, 2025.

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
CARs Part IX is the second-newest part of CARs and apply to everything related to RPAS’.
Canada is one of the world leaders in BVLOS operations in terms of regulations.
Prior to 2019, all drone operations happened with an SFOC (Part 9 of CARs did not exist).
L1C operations are covered under CAR 901 operations. Public safety organizations will be covered in CAR 903 under the SFOC process.
2019:
Canada enacted BVLOS operations under Part IX, allowing people to do routine BVLOS operations with RPAS less than 25kg.
2020:
Transport Canada started to develop low-risk BVLOS regulations.
There was a campaign and media show to present to the public and collect information.
They used the SFOC process as a learning ground.
The work done in Phase 1 UTM trials was learning for operators, Transport Canada, and NAV CANADA.
A lot of the lessons learned got converted into the L1C regulations.
2023:
Transport Canada proposed updates for low-risk BVLOS operations, released it to the public, and took in feedback.
2025: 
Transport Canada used the feedback to make revisions and release a new body of regulations.
These new regulations are what we’re covering in this L1C course.

Activities
N/A

title: CARs Part IX Structure
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Subpart 1 is CARs 901 and talks about all the different operating conditions.
We will discuss the following divisions the most:
Division 6: L1C Operations
Division 12: RPAS Operator Certificate
Division 5: Advanced Operations (the changes to this section grant a lot of additional privileges)
We will not delve deeply into Subpart 3.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX - Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
Advertised Event: An outdoor event that is advertised to the general public, including a concert, festival, market, or sporting event
BVLOS Operation: An operation of an RPA that is not in VLOS, but does not include an extended VLOS operation or a sheltered operation
Contingency Procedures: The procedures to be followed to address conditions that could lead to an unsafe situation
Contingency Volume: The area immediately surrounding the flight geography within which contingency procedures are intended to be used to return an RPA to the flight geography or safely terminate the flight
Note: Some definitions have been moved to Part I of CARs.

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
It is important that everyone is familiar with these definitions, so everyone is speaking the same language.
Advertised Event: 
An event is considered advertised if you’re making a public invitation. For example, a wedding would not count.
Transport Canada has a mandate to protect the public in open air events.
A drone under 250g still needs an SFOC to operate at an advertised event.
BVLOS Operation:
When the drone is not in VLOS of the pilot or visual observer.
BVLOS does not include an extended VLOS operation.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
Extended VLOS Operation: An operation of an RPA that is not in VLOS but during which unaided visual contact is maintained with the airspace in which the aircraft is operating in a manner sufficient to detect conflicting air traffic and other hazards and take action to avoid them
Flight Geography: The area within which an RPA is intended to fly for a specific operation
Fly-away: In respect of an RPA, an interruption or loss of the command and control link, such that the pilot no longer being able to control the aircraft and the aircraft no longer following its preprogrammed procedures or operating in a predictable or planned manner

notes:
Instructor Notes
There is another slide with an extended explanation of Extended VLOS Operation.
Flight Geography will be covered when talking about the SORA.

Key Points
Extended VLOS is not BVLOS under CARs Part 9.
People often confuse an RTL event with a fly-away.
If the drone is doing what it was programmed to do after loss of control, it is not a fly-away.
A fly-away is when the RPAS departs the operational volume and you can no longer control it.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
Ground Risk Buffer: The area immediately surrounding the contingency volume that, when measured horizontally from the perimeter of the contingency volume, is at least equal to the planned maximum altitude of the RPA for the flight
Mandatory Action: The inspection, repair, or modification of an RPAS that is necessary to prevent an unsafe or potentially unsafe condition
Medium Remotely Piloted Aircraft: An RPA that has an operating weight of more than 25 kg (55 lbs) but not more than 150 kg (331 lbs)
Small Remotely Piloted Aircraft: An RPA that has an operating weight of at least 250 g (0.55 lb) but not more than 25 kg (55 lbs)

notes:
Instructor Notes
Ground Risk Buffer will be discussed when operational volume is discussed.

Key Points
Mandatory Action: Something the operator and maintenance personnel are going to have to do to maintain the airworthiness of the aircraft.
Medium RPAS: New category that is allowed under your advanced privileges. Organizations can operate an RPAS between 25kg and 150kg without an SFOC.
There are different privileges and safety assurances depending on whether pilots are operating a small or medium RPAS.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
Operational Volume: The area that is composed of the flight geography, contingency volume, and ground risk buffer
Payload: A system, object, or collection of objects, including a slung load, that is on board or is otherwise connected to an RPA but that is not required for flight
Populated Area: An area with more than 5 people per square kilometre
RPAS Ground School Instruction: Instructor-led training given to 1 or more persons, delivered in-person or virtually, and provided through an organized program of lectures, homework, or self-paced study
RPAS Operations Manual: The manual established by an RPAS operator under section 901.217

notes:
Instructor Notes
Operational Volume will be discussed when flight operations is covered.

Key Points
Payload: Anything that does not contribute to the operation of the aircraft (e.g., camera, delivery items)
CARs Part 9 has clear definitions of what populated, unpopulated, and sparely populated areas are.
RPAS Ground School Instruction: This course
RPAS Operations Manual (ROM): This is new. People that have been doing SFOCs most likely already have a ROM, because it is one of the ways to meet the requirements (not the only one). Under L1C, Transport Canada required RPAS operators to have a ROM in addition to the license.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
RPAS Operator: The holder of an RPAS Operator CertificateNote: Not the same as RPAS pilot.
RPAS Operator Certificate: A certificate issued under section 901.214
Sheltered Operation: An operation of an RPA that is not in VLOS and during which the aircraft remains at less than 200 ft (61 m), measured horizontally, from a building or structure and at an altitude no greater than 100 ft (30 m) above that building or structure
Operating Weight: The weight of an RPA at any point during a flight, including any payload and any safety equipment that is on board or otherwise connected to the aircraft

notes:
Instructor Notes
Sheltered operations will be further discussed in a later slide.

Key Points
Under the L1C, RPAS operations look more like a small airline with structure, organizational control, maintenance procedures, etc. That group (holder of the RPAS Operator Certificate) is referred to as the RPAS operator, instead of the pilot.
The operating weight is different from the maximum takeoff weight of an aircraft, which is a defined limitation that comes from the manufacturer.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division I – General Provisions

content.text:
Sparsely Populated Area: An area with more than 5 but not more than 25 people per square kilometre
Visual Line-of-sight (VLOS): Unaided visual contact maintained with an RPA in a manner sufficient to maintain control of the aircraft, know its location, and scan the airspace in which it is operating to detect conflicting air traffic and other hazards and take action to avoid them
Visual Observer: A crew member who is trained to assist the pilot in ensuring the safe conduct of a flight

notes:
Instructor Notes
In the in-person in-flight review, participants will need to state what the population density is for the proposed area of flight operations.

Key Points
Sparsely Populated Area: The 2021 Census by Statistics Canada is the current standard for assessing this.
VLOS: Pilots must know the drone’s location and see the drone close enough to be able to control it. There is no fixed limit, but this is typically 800 m to 1.2 km based on the size of the drone and meteorological conditions.
Visual Observer: It is important that this person is trained.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division II – 900.8 Emergency Security Perimeter

content.text: No person shall operate an RPA over or within the security perimeter established by a public authority in response to an emergency, unless an operation is conducted in the service of a public authority.

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Police, firefighters, and other public authority have a legal right to operate drones within an emergency security perimeter.
There is no CAR on security perimeter for manned aviation.
These security perimeter rules also apply to microdrones.

Activities
Discussion: What physically delineates a security perimeter? What are the challenges in identifying the security perimeter to apply this CAR?

title: Part IX – Division II – 900.09 General Operating and Flight Rules
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Commercial air service used to refer to CARs Part 7 (Air Canada, West Jet, etc.). Now, CARs acknowledges that drones can perform commercial air services.
Operators don’t need an SFOC if they’re a non-resident/non-Canadian and operating under a free trade agreement.
When operators haul goods, they need a CTA license, and the Canadian government will get a tax cut.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 900.14 to 900.20 RPAS Registration
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There have been no significant changes with RPAS registration.
However, under the new regulations, operators can register a drone above 25 kg into the drone management portal.

Activities
N/A

title: CARs Part IV – Subpart I

content.text: Small and medium RPAS

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
This section covers the basic and advanced privileges.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 901.19 General Operating & Flight RulesFitness of Crew Members

content.text:
No fatigued or unfit crew
No RPAS operation until 12 hours after consumption of alcohol, or under the influence of alcohol
No using any drug that impairs judgement

notes:
Instructor Notes
Reference the organization’s drug and alcohol policy.

Key Points
This applies to all CARs 901 operations: basic, advanced, L1C.
Even if a crew member has a prescription, they cannot be under the influence of drugs that impair judgement (e.g., medicinal marijuana). 
There is no medical license required to be a L1C pilot; however, they must complete a self-declaration stating they are fit to fly and not under the influence. 
Crew members are responsible for knowing and following their organizations drug and alcohol policy, including appliable testing that may apply.

Activities
Discussion: What are the current organizational policies towards drugs and alcohol? Do they meet the above regulation?

title: Part IV – Division III – 901.24 General Operating & Flight Rules Pre-Flight Information

content.text:
Pilot to be familiar with:
Site survey
RPAS declaration
Qualification of all crew members

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
When accepting an operation under CARs 901, ensure
The site survey is valid (even if operators do not do it themselves).
The RPAS has a safety assurance and declaration.
All crew members are qualified.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 901.26 Horizontal Distances (VLOS)
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
To be near a person/over a person means different things depending on the size of the RPAS.
Small RPAS: 30 m horizontally and 5 m vertically
Medium RPAS: 152 m horizontally 

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 901.27 Site Survey
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
The site survey requirements have changed under the new regulations.
For BLVOS operations, pilots are required to know their lateral distances from populated areas (population density).
Find these densities in the NRCAN Drone Site Selection Tool or the Statistics Canada population density by dissemination area.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 901.34 Minimum Weather Conditions

content.text: Note: Operations outside of these conditions require an SFOC.

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There is different criteria for these four different categories, which can cause confusion.
You must operate in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions.
For an mRPAS, if the visibility is less than 4 statute miles, the drone can’t be farther than half of that distance from you. This can be difficult to calculate.
You can fly outside of these weather conditions with an SFOC.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – 901.35 Icing

content.text:
...icing conditions are observed, are reported to exist or are likely to be encountered…unless the aircraft is equipped…
…frost, ice or snow adhering to any of the critical surfaces…

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
A critical surface is anything that contributes to the lifting agents.
It’s especially important to ensure there is no frost, ice, etc. on bigger drones.
“Icing conditions” could include a weather alert for freezing rain.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division III – Use of First-Person View Devices 901.38

content.text:
…unless a visual observer maintains unaided visual contact with the airspace…
FPV Device: Generates and transmits a streaming video image to a control station display or monitor, giving the pilot of an RPA the illusion of flying the aircraft from an onboard pilot’s perspective

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
The definition of FPV Device changed with the new regulations. 
Goggles are not required to be considered using an FPV device.
An RC model that has an RC controller with no video feed is not an FPV device.
If a pilot is so focused on the video that they are no longer monitoring the airspace, bring in a visual observer.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division IV – Basic Operations
notes:
Instructor Notes
Example: If there are any Model Aeronautics Association of Canada (MAAC) members (i.e., model aircraft clubs), those clubs must operate at least under basic RPAS operations.

Key Points
Basic operations have had no significant change under the new regulations.
These are very basis requirements to operate in basic RPAS operations.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IV – Division V – Advanced Operations
notes:
Instructor Notes
Safety assurances are covered in depth in the Systems slide deck.

Key Points
There have been significant changes to advanced operations under the new regulations (see slide for changes).
A safety assurance can also be called an RPAS declaration.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division V – 901.74 Advanced Operations Extended VLOS

content.text: Pilot & GCS
VO
Small RPAS
2 NM

Pilot and control station are located at the site for take-off and recovery
RPA within 2 NM of pilot, VO, and control station
RPA is always greater than 100 ft (30 m) horizontally from any person
VO maintains unaided visual contact with the airspace
Below 400 ft AGL
VO has basic RPAS certificate or better

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Extended VLOS is not the same as BVLOS.
Extended VLOS:
Drone must be within 2 NM of the pilot, visual observer, and the ground station.
People planning to run drones in boxes remotely will not be able to do Extended VLOS, because the drone will not be within 2 NM of the ground station.
RPAS must stay more than 30m horizontally from any person not involved in the operation, even if your drone has a safety assurance to fly over people. The CAR supersedes that safety assurance. 
Extended VLOS is very good for site surveys in unpopulated areas, but BVLOS is better for flying in a public area.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division V – 901.74 Advanced Ops Sheltered Operation

content.text: Small RPAS

Pilot and control station are located at the site for take-off and landing
RPA within 2 NM of pilot and control station
RPA is always greater than 100 ft (30 m) horizontally from any person
Below 400 ft AGL
100ft
200 ft
Pilot
2 NM

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
A sheltered operation may or may not be considered a BVLOS operation.
The drone must be within 100 ft vertically and 200 ft horizontally from a building, because there is almost no chance that a helicopter will also be in that airspace.
This gives the pilot the ability to inspect roofs, bridges, power lines, etc. 
The pilot does not need a visual observer.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division VI – L1C

content.text:
Applicability (901.87)
NRCAN DSST-2

notes:
Instructor Notes
There will be a slide later on how to use the NRCAN DSSST-2 tool.

Key Points
Use the NRCAN DSST-2 tool, because they’ve done the calculation on population density and airspace.
If the NRCAN map is unshaded, go fly. If it is shaded, they likely need an SFOC.
There are a few conditions (see the slide).

Activities
Practice Question: What is the definition of an unpopulated area and a sparsely populated area?
Answer: An unpopulated area has fewer than five people. A sparsely populated area has between five and 25 people.

Practice Question: Whose data set is used to determine these populated areas?
Answer: Statistics Canada most recent Census data

title: Division VI – L1C Operations

content.text:
3P approach:
Pilot: Level 1 Complex (901.89)
Procedure: RPAS Operator Certificate (901.88)
Platform: Declaration (901.95)

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There is a 3P approach to L1C operations: Pilot, Platform, Procedures.
When setting up an organization, it’s important for the operator to ask if they have all three Ps.
The pilot refers to the L1C pilot certificate.
The platform refers to the safety assurances that must be in place.
The procedures refer to the RPAS Operator Certificate, operations manual, procedures, checklists, deconfliction, etc.

Activities
N/A

title: Division VI – L1C Pilot

content.text:
18 years of age
Hold a L1C certificate
Meet the recency requirements (24 months)
Pilot certificate and recency demonstration are easily accessible during operations
Note: There is no requirement for a medical certificate

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There are requirements to be a L1C pilot. 
People often forget the recency requirement:
Transport Canada needs to know operators are up-to-date on the regulations. This L1C course requalifies participants for the recurrence. The easiest way to get requalified is the self-paced study from Transport Canada that comes with a study guide telling participants where to find the answers. When they fill out the questionnaire and keep it with them, they’re considered current.

Activities
N/A

title: Division VI – L1C Procedures

content.text:
RPAS Operator Certificate
Issued to individual or organization (the operator) through the DMP
Operator requires an RPAS Operations Manual (901.217) that includes:
Roles and responsibilities of crew members
Roles and responsibilities of maintenance personnel
Safety processes (901.218)
Normal and emergency procedures (901.23)
Training syllabus

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
An individual or a corporation can hold the RPAS Operator Certificate. 
To hold an RPAS Operator Certificate they need to: 
Be a Canadian or a Canadian organization
Have an RPAS Operations Manual in place that includes a safety policy, procedures, training, etc. 
Operators must administer yearly recurrent training to all crew members before they fly under the L1C operator certificate.

Activities
N/A

title: Division VI – L1C Platform
notes:
Instructor Notes
Reference the list using this link: https://tc.canada.ca/en/aviation/drone-safety/learn-rules-you-fly-your-drone/choosing-right-drone-advanced-operations

Key Points
There are three different operational categories of drones.
After November 4th, 2025, Transport Canada updated the public list of safety assurances from manufactures to include BVLOS operations.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division VI – L1C Modification of RPAS

content.text:
Demonstrate Standard 922 is met
Perform in accordance with manufacturer instructions

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
If you modify your drone, you will not be able to exercise these privileges unless you:
Demonstrate Standard 922 is met
Perform the modifications in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions
If modifications are performed in accordance with manufacturer instructions, operators do not need to demonstrate to Transport Canada that Standard 922 is met.
If the manufacturer does not provide instructions on how to modify their drone, the operator must prove to Transport Canada they still meet Standard 922. It is recommended not to modify drones unless the manufacturer provides instructions on how to do so.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division VI – L1C Detect & Avoid Requirements

content.text:
If RPAS does not have onboard capability for DAA:
Standard 923 (human) Vision-Based Detect and Avoid

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There are six new standards a manufacturer can make a declaration against.
The standard against DAA assumes the manufacturer is going to build an onboard DAA system, which is not always going to be the case.
If the manufacturer does not have a DAA system, it’s acceptable to do BVLOS integrations using human-based DAA. This is covered in Standard 923, VO DAA.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division VI – L1C Standard 923 Vision-Based DAA

content.text: Pilot & GCS
VO
4 NM
2 NM

Sun:
Outside the quadrant, greater than 45 degrees above horizon, or
below the horizon

Visibility = 3 SM+

Ceiling = 1,000 ft AGL+

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
VO DAA looks a lot like the standard scenario for low-risk BVLOS operations.
The conditions of Standard 923:
Drone can be flown upwards of 4 NM away from the pilot and the ground control station.
Drone must within 2 NM of the VO.
Pilot must have a visibility of greater than 3 SM.
Ceiling must be greater than 1000 ft AGL.
Sun can’t be in pilot’s eyes when they’re looking at the airspace.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division VI – L1C Standard 923 Vision-Based DAA

content.text:
How to judge visibility:
Nearest METAR
Measure section lines of farms (¼ section = ½ SM)
Tower in the distance
How to judge ceilings:
Nearest METAR
Towers
Mountains
How to judge visual obstructions:
Outstretched arm with 4 horizontal fingers stacked on the horizon
Roberts, 2007.

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
How to judge visibility:
Section lines (2 section lines is 1 SM)
Aviation-based weather stations 
Look at a tower in the distance (this is how the weather reporters at NAV CANADA assess visibility)
How to judge ceilings:
Clouds hitting the top of rolling hills
Aviation-based weather station
Look at a tower (they’re usually 200-300 ft)

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division X – Training & Flight Review

content.text:
Standard 921 – Remotely Pilot Aircraft – CARs
Basic pilot certificate
Advanced pilot certificate
Flight reviewer rating
Recency requirements
Declared training provider requirements (TP15530 TP15263)
L1C pilot certificate

notes:
Instructor Notes
While this is not a critical slide, it is useful to show participants that there are defined standards for training organizations to follow.

Key Points
This standard:
Covers training for all RPAS pilot certificates
Tells you what it takes to be a declared training provider

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division X – Training & Flight Review

content.text:
Flight reviewer (901.176):
Advanced or L1C endorsed with flight reviewer rating (as appropriate)
Affiliated with a ‘declared’ training provider

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
To be a flight reviewer for L1C, reviewers must:
Have the L1C pilot certificate
Be affiliated with a declared training provider

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division X – Training & Flight Review

content.text:
Retaking of Exam (901.178)
24-hour period to review

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Participants must wait 24 hours to take the L1C exam again if they fail on the first attempt.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division X – Training & Flight Review

content.text: Flight Reviewer’s Guide TP 15395

notes:
Instructor Notes
There are standards flight reviewers must follow.
If there are students in the classroom who will become L1C flight reviewers, this document should be downloaded and possibly discussed. 

Key Points
This is the guiding document for flight reviews.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XI – RPAS Declarations

content.text:
Responsibilities of the declarant (Manufacturer):
‘Declaration’ to the Minister assuring target levels of safety for the required standards
Maintenance program
Mandatory actions for continued compliance/airworthiness
RPAS Operations Manual (pilot’s handbook)
Record keeping substantiating declaration
Service difficulty reports (PVD)
Annual reporting to the Minister (PVD)

notes:
Instructor Notes
This will be covered again in the Systems slide deck.

Key Points
These are the new declarations in place (see table).
Safety and Reliability and Environmental Envelope have dollar signs next to them, because they are going to be very costly standards for a manufacturer to achieve.
Every country is going to have a different safety assurance.
When operators purchase a drone with a safety assurance, they know that the manufacturer has met all the conditions (maintenance program, mandatory actions, etc.).
Service Difficulty Report: If an operator has a problem with the drone that is a manufacturing or design fault, the manufacturer must keep that on record.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XI – Operational Categories Requiring Safety Assurances
notes:
Instructor Notes
These are the new operational categories, and the safety assurances that are required in each category.

Key Points
The columns are organized by VLOS versus BVLOS.
The grouping of rows is RPAS maximum take-off weight.
Each box groups together the specific ‘standards’ against which a manufacturer must make a safety assurance.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XI – RPAS Declarations

content.text:
Declarations are required for lower ground risk:
sRPAS over/near people and BVLOS greater than 1 km from populated areas
Pre-validated Declarations (PVD) are required for higher ground risk:
mRPAS over/near people
sRPAS over sparsely populated areas/less than 1 km from populated areas

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Operators only need to worry about declarations.
Manufacturers worry about PVD, which is a higher level of declaration.
If a drone has a safety assurance from a manufacturer that is referred to as a PVD, operators will have a few more privileges. This means that the manufacturer has done a lot more work with Transport Canada to obtain that PVD.
The first question operators should ask when a salesman is trying to sell a drone is: What are you doing for the safety assurance?

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate

content.text:
Eligibility to hold (901.213):
Canadian citizen or permanent resident of Canada
Government of Canada or ‘agent of’
Corporation or entity formed under laws of Canada or a province
Commercial air service or not?

notes:
Instructor Notes
If the organization to which the students belong already has an RPAS Operator Certificate, the instructor can show the operating certificate to the students.

Key Points
The 3Ps are pilot, platform, and procedures. This falls under the third P, procedures.
Everyone that wants to exercise L1C privileges has to do so under an RPAS Operator Certificate.
You apply for an RPAS Operator Certificate through the drone management portal as an individual or corporation. 
When you apply for this certificate, you are making a declaration to Transport Canada that you have the policies and processes in place to be an RPAS operator.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate

content.text:
General Conditions (901.216):
Organizational structure
Maintain a training program
RPAS that is proper for the type of operation
RPAS maintenance IAW with manufacturer’s instructions
Notify Minister of organizational changes

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
To obtain an RPAS Operator Certificate, they must:
Define an organizational structure.
Maintain a training program (including annual recurrency training with record keeping).
Have an RPAS that is proper for the types of operations.
Outline their maintenance plan.
Have a process to notify the Minister of organizational changes.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate RPAS Operations Manual

content.text:
901.217:
Roles and responsibilities of crew
Roles and responsibilities of all operational and maintenance personnel
Safety program/processes
Normal and emergency procedures
Training program and personnel qualifications

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Operators are required by law to have an RPAS Operations Manual before applying for an RPAS Operator Certificate.
They don’t have to have an SMS, but they do have to have a safety program.
Visual observers must be trained under their training program.

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate (Hierarchy)
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
It is very important to have a named Accountable Executive, so Transport Canada knows who holds the ultimate responsibility.
The only other role that must have a person named is the Person Responsible for Maintenance.

Activities
Discussion: If your organization has an RPAS Operator Certificate, can you draft the organizational structure?                    OR If your organization does not have an RPAS Operator Certificate, can you draft a possible organizational structure?

title: Part IX – Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate Manual Requirements
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Operators must have the following in their RPAS Operations Manual:
Safety program (This does not need to be an SMS. It can be external to the RPAS manual, but that is not recommended from a record keeping standpoint.)
MCM
Training program
Record keeping details
Normal and emergency procedures (e.g., fly-aways)
NASA has compiled 30 years of research on how to develop checklists, etc.

Activities
N/A

title: CARs Part IX – Subpart III

content.text: Special Flight Operations (903)

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
There will still be times operators need to get an SFOC (e.g., flying in the shaded areas of DSST-2).

Activities
N/A

title: Part IX – Subpart III – Special Flight Operations (SFOC)
notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Biggest change in SFOCs: If operators are flying a sub 250 g drone at an advertised event, they need an SFOC.
Now, operators do not have to get an SFOC for a lot of low-risk BVLOS operations, due to the L1C.
SFOCs are now categorized into low, medium, and high complexity.
A foreign operator used to need an SFOC, but they can now operate as an agent under an operator’s RPAS Operator Certificate.
Operators can only have a drone registered in one country at a time.

Activities
N/A

title: Part II – SOR/2025-70 – Service FeesCanada Gazette

content.text:
Previous and current fees for the following services are available on the Canada Gazette website:
Drone registration
L1C operations exam
L1C operations pilot certificate
Pre-validated declarations
RPAS Operator Certificate
SFOC – Very low, medium, and high complexity
Amendment to an existing  SFOC

notes:
Instructor Notes
A breakdown of previous and current fees for the listed services can be found on the Canada Gazette website: https://gazette.gc.ca/rp-pr/p2/2025/2025-03-26/html/sor-dors70-eng.html 

Key Points
Operators now need to pay for an SFOC.
High-complexity SFOCs are very expensive ($2000).

Activities
N/A

title: Division XII – RPAS Operator Certificate ROM Guidance

content.text: Guidance on Manual Development for Remotely Piloted Aircraft System Operator Certificate (RPOC)
Professional Compliance & Writing Services

notes:
Instructor Notes
N/A

Key Points
Scriptorium can help Operators build an RPAS Operations Manual.

Activities
N/A
